scaling_roadmap.detail: Scaling roadmap documents exist. Plans have been written but quality and completeness have not been independently validated.

pm2_optimization.detail: PM2 optimization plans exist (cluster mode, instance counts, memory limits). None have been applied or tested.

docker_optimization.detail: Docker optimization plans exist (resource limits, restart policies, log rotation). None have been applied or tested.

postgresql_optimization.detail: PostgreSQL optimization plans exist (connection pooling, query optimization, indexing). None have been applied. Current connection pool limits, query performance, and index coverage are unknown.

redis_optimization.detail: Redis optimization plans exist (maxmemory policy, persistence config, eviction strategy). Current Redis memory usage, hit ratio, and eviction behavior are unknown.

load_testing.detail: No load test has ever been executed against any Wheeler service. No baseline throughput, latency, or concurrency data exists. This is the single largest gap in the scalability domain.

capacity_planning.detail: No verified capacity ceilings for: max concurrent PM2 processes, max Docker containers, max database connections, max API requests/sec, max WebSocket connections, max file descriptors per service.

horizontal_scaling.detail: All services run on a single AIOPS host. No load balancer configuration. No multi-instance capability. No service discovery mechanism for horizontal scaling.

performance_baselines.detail: No baseline performance metrics exist for any service. Unknown: p50/p95/p99 latency, requests/second, error rates, resource consumption per request.

auto_scaling.detail: No auto-scaling mechanisms of any kind. No horizontal pod autoscaling, no vertical scaling triggers, no load-based resource adjustment. Note: auto-scaling may not be needed at current scale — this is a documentation gap, not necessarily an implementation gap.

phase_1_baseline.description: Establish performance baseline for all public/internal endpoints

phase_2_stress.targets: same as phase_1, prioritized by criticality
metrics: degradation point (first metric to breach SLA)
failure mode (graceful degradation vs hard crash)

phase_3_endurance.description: Sustained load test at 70% of breaking point

cpu.action: Evaluate PM2 instance scaling or service offloading

memory.action: Identify memory leak, increase swap, or add RAM

disk.action: Log rotation, Docker cleanup, or disk expansion

error_rate.action: Investigate and potentially rollback recent deploy

scaling_milestones.description: Performance baseline documented for all public endpoints
Load test completed at 5x baseline, bottlenecks identified
All capacity ceilings documented with rationale
Top 3 optimization actions from plans executed and measured
Horizontal scaling feasibility documented for each service

actions: Run baseline performance tests (wrk or ab) against all public endpoints
Document: requests/sec, p50/p95/p99 latency, error rate
Record resource consumption at baseline (CPU, memory, disk I/O)
Execute 2x load test, identify first bottleneck
Execute 5x load test, identify breaking point
Document failure mode (graceful vs hard crash)
Define capacity limits based on test results
Apply top 3 optimization actions from existing plans
Document horizontal scaling feasibility per service
Conduct endurance test (30 min sustained load)

projected_scores.maximum_blocked_by: Full scalability proof requires sustained production traffic patterns, canary deployments under load, and multi-instance architecture — all of which require the ecosystem to be live first. Scalability is inherently validated in production.

risk_assessment.detail: At current scale (single host, moderate load), the system is stable. The risk is latent: without baselines and load testing, we do not know where the breaking point is. This means the system could fail unexpectedly under load with no advance warning. Priority: establish baselines immediately, load test within 14 days.
